# Form control parts

<FeatureLinks mdn="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/search?q=%3A%3Afile-selector-button%20CSS" caniuse="https://caniuse.com/?search=%3A%3Afile-selector-button" status="Forms/Pseudo" label="Reference links" />

## Plain-language explanation

Style file buttons, dropdown arrows, check marks, slider tracks, and other parts.

## Representative syntax

```css
::file-selector-button, ::picker-icon, ::checkmark, ::thumb, ::track, ::fill
```

## Demo

<CssDemo title="Form control parts" css-b64="" code-b64="" html-b64="" caption="The form demo keeps native controls and shows how CSS can shape the interaction surface without losing semantics." lang="en-US" badge="Practical demo" />

## When to use it

<div class="usage-grid">
<section>
  <h3>Best fit</h3>
  <p>Use Form control parts in interactive controls when you need to make native controls feel integrated without removing their built-in behavior. It usually gives the stylesheet a clearer job than extra classes, wrappers, or script.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Example</h3>
  <p>A preference panel can keep native validation and keyboard handling while matching the product accent and focus style.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Watch out</h3>
  <p>After styling, test disabled, focus, invalid, high-contrast, and keyboard-only states. Those are where custom control styling usually breaks.</p>
</section>
</div>

## Source metadata

- Category: Forms, Controls, and Interactive UI

- Representative syntax: `::file-selector-button, ::picker-icon, ::checkmark, ::thumb, ::track, ::fill`
